title: Green Discharge in Pregnant Cats: Emergency Guide & Next Steps
meta_desc: If your pregnant cat has green discharge, it could be a medical emergency. Learn what it means for her and her kittens and why you must act quickly.
content_html:
TL;DR: Green discharge (lochia) in a pregnant cat is an emergency if a kitten is not born immediately after it appears. Contact a veterinarian right away if more than 30 minutes pass without a birth, as this indicates a life-threatening complication for the mother and kittens.

What does it mean if my pregnant cat has green discharge?

Green discharge, often referred to as lochia, is a fluid produced when a placenta separates from the uterine wall. While this is a normal part of the birthing process, its timing is critical. If the discharge appears and is not immediately followed by the birth of a kitten, it suggests that the kittens' oxygen and nutrient supply may be at risk.

Is green discharge in my pregnant cat a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High.
  • If your cat is showing green discharge and has not yet produced a kitten, seek immediate veterinary care.
  • It is a medical emergency if more than 30 minutes have passed since the discharge appeared without a birth.
  • This often indicates dystocia (difficult birth) or placental separation, which can be life-threatening for the kittens and the mother.

How can taking a photo of the discharge help the veterinarian with triage?

  • Take a clear photo of the discharge to show the emergency clinical team upon arrival or during a call.
  • A visual aid allows the team to assess the specific color, viscosity, and volume of the fluid.
  • Photos help vets determine if the discharge is normal lochia or a sign of infection, such as pyometra.
  • Providing a photo helps the clinic prepare the surgical suite or treatment room before you even arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

During normal parturition, vaginal discharge can be clear, serous to hemorrhagic, or green (uteroverdin). However, a dark green vaginal discharge before the delivery of any neonates is abnormal and indicates placental separation, warranting immediate veterinary attention. Dystocia should be considered an emergency if there is abnormal vulvar discharge (e.g., frank blood, dark green discharge before any neonates are born). A sterile digital vaginal examination should be performed to evaluate the patency of the birth canal and the position and presentation of the fetus(es).

Chapter: Reproduction

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2227)

faqs.question: Is green discharge ever normal during cat pregnancy?
faqs.answer: It is only considered normal if a kitten is delivered within 10 to 20 minutes of the discharge appearing. If no kitten is born shortly after, it indicates a problem.

faqs.question: What should I do if my cat is acting normal but has green discharge?
faqs.answer: Even if your cat seems comfortable, green discharge without the arrival of a kitten is a physical sign of placental detachment. You should still contact a veterinarian immediately.

faqs.question: What are other signs of birthing complications?
faqs.answer: Other red flags include more than 20 minutes of intense straining without a birth, more than two hours between kittens, or a foul-smelling discharge.
